title: Is the Modality Gap a Bug or a Feature? A Robustness Perspective
abstract: Many modern multi-modal models (e.g. CLIP) seek an embedding space in which the two modalities are aligned. Somewhat surprisingly, almost all existing models show a strong modality gap: the distribution of images is well-separated from the distribution of texts in the shared embedding space. Despite a series of recent papers on this topic, it is still not clear why this gap exists nor whether closing the gap in post-processing will lead to better performance on downstream tasks. In this paper we show that under certain conditions, minimizing the contrastive loss yields a representation in which the two modalities are separated by a global gap vector that is orthogonal to their embeddings. We also show that under these conditions the modality gap is monotonically related to robustness: decreasing the gap does not change the clean accuracy of the models but makes it less likely that a model will change its output when the embeddings are perturbed. Our experiments show that for many real-world VLMs we can significantly increase robustness by a simple post-processing step that moves one modality towards the mean of the other modality, without any loss of clean accuracy.

sections.heading: 1Introduction
sections.text:
Foundation models are a common and successful approach to solving a variety of problems - models are trained on extremely large datasets and then adapted to other tasks either by fine tuning or zero shot application, under the assumption that they learned a meaningful embedding space. A specific and popular class of these models are contrastive multi-modal models. These are trained to learn a shared embedding space for different data types such as images and texts by aligning pairs of similar instances from the two modalities via a contrastive loss [ 30 , 32 , 41 ] . These shared embedding spaces, specifically that of CLIP [ 20 ] , are commonly used for various tasks such as zero shot classification, text-to-image retrieval, text to image generation, and more.

The success of multi-modal models on a wide range of different applications (e.g. text-to-image generative models [ 22 ] ), might imply that they have successfully solved the optimization problem on which they were trained, yet some open questions persist. One of these regards the "modality gap" [ 11 ] - a phenomenon in which the two modalities are embedded into linearly separable regions of the unit hypersphere. Figure 1 a shows projections of CLIP’s embedding of the MS-COCO validation set onto its first three principal components. Clearly, the two modalities are well separated in embedding space. This widely observed phenomenon across models and data types contradicts the training objective which aims to make similar texts and images perfectly overlap in embedding space—an objective known as "alignment" [ 34 ] .

Recent works have tried to explain the cause for the formation of the gap, suggesting either information imbalance between images and texts (i.e. when a single caption is appropriate for multiple images) [ 25 ] or the use of contrastive loss in cases when the effective dimension of each modality is small ("dimensionality collapse") [ 43 ] , but a clear explanation remains elusive. Concurrently, investigation into the downstream effects of the gap has also failed to reach a clear conclusion [ 11 , 27 , 5 , 12 , 21 , 43 ] . Figure ˜ 2 presents the application of various multi-modal models on common downstream tasks. The performance of these models varies non-monotonously when adjusting the gap by moving the modality means towards each other, implying no clear relation between performance and the size of the gap.

Another open topic regarding multi-modal models and deep learning in general focuses on the robustness of models to small variations in their input. Various works have shown that despite their large training corpus and the numerous augmentations used during their training, the performance of these models on downstream tasks is sensitive to small variations in the input such as single pixel shifts [ 28 ] and caption rephrasing [ 44 , 9 ] . Figure 1 b demonstrates this brittleness. When trying to classify an image as a dog or a frog using CLIP, the model outputs different predictions depending on the the two texts with which the image is compared, even if the change is semantically meaningless.

In this work we establish a direct link between these two lines of work. In Sec. ˜ 3 we expand on [ 43 ] and prove that under realistic assumptions, multi-modal models learn an embedding space containing a modality gap due to their initialization and the dynamics induced by the contrastive loss. We continue to prove that the size of the gap is correlated with the robustness of these models, i.e. their ability to perform consistently under small changes to the embedding space. Our theory, along with empirical verification on real models and datasets, with both controlled (i.e. Gaussian) and real noise (i.e. text rephrasing), leads us to conclude that if robustness is a desired property of the models then the modality gap is indeed a bug caused by an interaction of the multi-modal contrastive loss and the initialization scheme.

Inspired by our theoretical findings, in Sec. ˜ 4 we present a simple, efficient algorithm to close the gap which can be applied as a post processing step when using the different models’ embeddings spaces. In Sec. ˜ 5 we demonstrate a use case of this algorithm in dealing with various noise models such as quantization and other real noise settings where the theoretical assumptions are not met.

sections.heading: 2Related Work
sections.text:
Multi-modal contrastive learning [ 41 ] is a method for learning a shared embedding space for different modalities e.g. images and texts. Models trained in this manner such as CLIP [ 20 ] and others [ 36 , 40 ] show impressive performance on downstream tasks, e.g. zero shot classification. This has led to widespread use of CLIP’s embedding space in various tasks, most notably in text to image generation [ 22 , 7 ] . While the contrastive loss [ 30 , 32 ] of multi-modal models pushes the modality pairs’ embeddings to be aligned and uniformly spread on the unit sphere [ 34 ] , in practice multi-modal models map the different modalities to distinct areas of the shared embedding space, creating a modality gap [ 11 ] .

Several explanations as to the formation of the gap were suggested, stemming from either the data used to train these models [ 11 , 25 ] , the inherent differences between text and images [ 25 ] or the training procedure [ 27 , 37 ] . Recently, Zhang et al. [ 43 ] have suggested that the gap is caused due to a combination of training with the contrastive loss and a dimensional collapse in the initial embeddings of the two modalities [ 6 ] . In this work we show that dimensionality collapse is neither necessary nor sufficient for the formation of the gap and we provide a more nuanced analysis of the dynamics of learning which cause the gap.

In addition to explaining the formation of the gap, previous works have also attempted to understand its effects on downstream performance. It has previously been shown that a large gap limits the ability to perform cross-modal tasks with uni-modal data [ 43 ] and is negatively correlated in specific settings of cross modal retrieval [ 12 , 15 ] . In general, however, recent work concludes that there is at most a weak effect of the gap on downstream performance [ 5 , 11 , 25 ] while other factors such as model and embedding size seem to have a stronger effect [ 25 ] . Some works even suggest maintaining the modality gap [ 21 , 5 ] , or show that it has no effect in specific downstream settings [ 42 ] . We build on these findings and and prove that the gap indeed has negative effect on downstream performance in terms of robustness.

Alongside the attempt to understand the gap and its implications, previous works suggested various methods for closing the gap. These were mainly motivated by failure modes in specific settings [ 16 , 12 ] , or by the inherent contradiction of the gap to the contrastive loss objective. Some works suggest closing the gap by altering the training procedure of multi-modal models with specific losses and augmentations [ 17 , 37 ] , while others include the training of models that implicitly close the gap as part of pipelines performing complex downstream tasks such as text to image generation [ 22 , 18 ] . Our work falls in line with these, but is applicable in a general setting - for any downstream task that relies on cross modal nearest neighbor retrieval, and with no additional training required.

A different line of work studies multi-modal models in the context of robustness [ 2 , 31 , 39 ] . Various works have shown that their performance on downstream tasks is sensitive to both textual variations [ 44 , 19 ] , adversarial attacks [ 23 ] , various image perturbations such as augmentations [ 19 ] , spurious correlations [ 33 ] , natural variations [ 28 ] and more [ 35 ] . These are tackled mainly via finetuning and retraining with different objectives or data [ 24 , 44 , 8 , 29 ] . Our theoretical work aims to enrich the understanding of these shortcomings by proving that a modality gap causes degrading in robustness.

sections.heading: 3.1Notation and Definitions
sections.text:
As in previous work, we assume two modalities that are embedded on the d d -dimensional unit hypersphere. Our embeddings are in the form of sample matrices 𝒳 ∈ ℝ N × d \mathcal{X}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times d} and 𝒴 ∈ ℝ M × d \mathcal{Y}\in\mathbb{R}^{M\times d} with modality means μ x = 1 N ​ ∑ x → ∈ 𝒳 x → \mu_{x}=\frac{1}{N}\sum_{\vec{x}\in\mathcal{X}}\vec{x} and μ y = 1 M ​ ∑ y → ∈ 𝒴 y → \mu_{y}=\frac{1}{M}\sum_{\vec{y}\in\mathcal{Y}}\vec{y} . Many downstream tasks rely on cross modal retrieval - finding the nearest neighbor of sample y → ∈ 𝒴 \vec{y}\in\mathcal{Y} among the samples in the other embedding 𝒳 \mathcal{X} by computing the ℓ 2 \ell_{2} distance between the two: NN ​ ( y → , 𝒳 ) = arg ​ min x → ∈ 𝒳 ⁡ ‖ x → − y → ‖ 2 \text{NN}(\vec{y},\mathcal{X})=\operatorname*{arg\,min}_{\vec{x}\in\mathcal{X}}\left\lVert\vec{x}-\vec{y}\right\rVert^{2} .

Since the embeddings lie on the unit hypersphere, the NN can equivalently be computed using cosine similarity.

We focus on models trained with the multi-modal contrastive loss. During training we are given a paired dataset { x i , y i } \{x_{i},y_{i}\} (e.g. image and matching caption) and minimize the following loss: ℒ ​ ( 𝒳 , 𝒴 ) = − 1 N ​ ∑ i = 1 N log ⁡ ℓ ​ ( x → i , 𝒴 ) − 1 N ​ ∑ j = 1 N log ⁡ ℓ ​ ( y → j , 𝒳 ) \mathcal{L}(\mathcal{X},\mathcal{Y})=-\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\log\ell(\vec{x}_{i},\mathcal{Y})-\frac{1}{N}\sum_{j=1}^{N}\log\ell(\vec{y}_{j},\mathcal{X}) (1) Where ℓ \ell contrasts an embedding with the other modality: ℓ ​ ( x → i , 𝒴 ) = e − ‖ x → i − y → i ‖ / τ ∑ y → j ∈ 𝒴 e − ‖ x → i − y → j ‖ / τ \ell(\vec{x}_{i},\mathcal{Y})=\frac{e^{-\left\lVert\vec{x}_{i}-\vec{y}_{i}\right\rVert/\tau}}{\sum_{\vec{y}_{j}\in\mathcal{Y}}e^{-\left\lVert\vec{x}_{i}-\vec{y}_{j}\right\rVert/\tau}} (2) with τ > 0 \tau>0 being the temperature parameter.

We follow previous works [ 42 ] and define, for a paired dataset the local gap as the vector between a pair from the two modalities: g → i = x → i − y → i \vec{g}_{i}=\vec{x}_{i}-\vec{y}_{i} (3) We also define the global gap [ 11 , 42 , 27 ] as the vector between the modality means: g → = 1 M ​ ∑ y → ∈ 𝒴 y → − 1 N ​ ∑ x → ∈ 𝒳 x → = μ y − μ x \vec{g}=\frac{1}{M}\sum_{\vec{y}\in\mathcal{Y}}\vec{y}-\frac{1}{N}\sum_{\vec{x}\in\mathcal{X}}\vec{x}=\mu_{y}-\mu_{x} (4) When the two modalities are balanced ( N = M N=M ), the global vector is the average of local vectors. Nevertheless, the global gap vector is well defined for any dataset consisting of two modalities, whether a bijective pairing exists or not.

sections.heading: 3.2Why Should a Global Gap Exist?
sections.text:
Since the contrastive loss rewards embeddings in which the representations in the two modalities are aligned [ 34 ] , the presence of a global gap in embeddings learned using such a loss is highly surprising. Indeed, it is easy to show that the global minimum of the contrastive loss is obtained when the representations are maximally aligned, i.e. ∀ i : x i = y i \forall i:x_{i}=y_{i} , entailing no local or global gap, i.e. g → i = 0 \vec{g}_{i}=0 . But it is also easy to see that there exist many other embeddings that achieve almost the same loss as the globally aligned embedding. Figure ˜ 3 shows an example. The lines are the local gap vectors g → i \vec{g}_{i} which show the correspondence between the two modalities: ideally we want circles and crosses that are connected to lie on top of each other in embedding space, meaning g → i = 0 \vec{g}_{i}=0 . As can be seen in the figure, the loss and the gradient can be made arbitrarily close to zero without perfect alignment: as long as the corresponding embeddings are closer than all other cross-modal pairs, the loss and the gradient will approach 0 . In particular, the solution in the bottom right (where there is a modality gap) achieves practically the same loss and gradient magnitude as the desired, perfectly aligned solution. Intriguingly, gradient descent sometimes seems to prefer these low-loss solutions over the global optimum. Figure ˜ 4 shows dynamics of points in low dimensions, in which we minimize the contrastive loss with respect to the embeddings coordinates. In both of these cases, training converges to a solution that has almost zero loss but for which a global gap vector remains.

Zhang et al. [ 43 ] postulate that dimensional collapse, i.e. when the embeddings’ variance within each modality is concentrated in relatively few dimensions, is the cause for the modality gap. But why does the modality gap happen when there is equal variance in all dimensions? Figures ˜ 4 and 5 show such cases - both embeddings are initialized by sampling from isotropic Gaussians, therefore maintaining equal variance in all dimensions even after normalization to the sphere. Still learning yields a representation with a global gap, even without dimensional collapse. Therefore, further investigation is needed.

We begin by defining: Q x ​ ( i , j ) \displaystyle Q^{x}(i,j) = e − ‖ x i − y j ‖ 2 / τ ∑ j ′ e − ‖ x i − y j ′ ‖ 2 / τ \displaystyle=\frac{e^{-\|x_{i}-y_{j}\|^{2}/\tau}}{\sum_{j^{\prime}}e^{-\|x_{i}-y_{j^{\prime}}\|^{2}/\tau}} Q y ​ ( i , j ) \displaystyle Q^{y}(i,j) = e − ‖ x i − y j ‖ 2 / τ ∑ i ′ e − ‖ x i ′ − y j ‖ 2 / τ \displaystyle=\frac{e^{-\|x_{i}-y_{j}\|^{2}/\tau}}{\sum_{i^{\prime}}e^{-\|x_{i^{\prime}}-y_{j}\|^{2}/\tau}} (5)

Which are known as the softmax of the logit matrix [ 20 ] . Q x Q^{x} can be thought of as a soft assignment matrix: for each x i x_{i} it measures the normalized affinity between x i x_{i} and all points y j y_{j} in the other modality while Q y Q^{y} does the same for each point y i y_{i} . By construction, the sum of the rows of Q x Q^{x} are equal to one, and the sum of the columns of Q y Q^{y} are equal to one.
Using these matrices the loss becomes: ℒ ​ ( 𝒳 , 𝒴 ) = − ( 1 N ​ ∑ i = 1 N log ⁡ Q x ​ ( i , i ) + log ⁡ Q y ​ ( i , i ) ) \mathcal{L}(\mathcal{X},\mathcal{Y})=-\left(\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\log Q^{x}(i,i)+\log Q^{y}(i,i)\right) (6)

The gradient of the loss can be written:

∂ ℒ ∂ y i ∝ ( x i − y i ) − ∑ k = 1 N Q x ​ ( k , i ) + Q y ​ ( k , i ) 2 ​ ( x k − y i ) \frac{\partial\mathcal{L}}{\partial y_{i}}\propto(x_{i}-y_{i})-\sum_{k=1}^{N}\frac{Q^{x}(k,i)+Q^{y}(k,i)}{2}(x_{k}-y_{i}) (7)

The gradient has two opposing effects: an attractive force ( x i − y i ) (x_{i}-y_{i}) pushes y i y_{i} towards it matching x i x_{i} and repulsive forces which push y i y_{i} away from x k x_{k} . The strength of the repulsive force is determined by ∑ k = 1 N Q x ​ ( k , i ) + Q y ​ ( k , i ) 2 = 1 2 ​ ( 1 + S i y ) \sum_{k=1}^{N}\frac{Q^{x}(k,i)+Q^{y}(k,i)}{2}=\frac{1}{2}(1+S_{i}^{y}) with S i y := ∑ k Q x ​ ( k , i ) S_{i}^{y}:=\sum_{k}Q^{x}(k,i) . Intuitively, this means that points for which S i y > 1 S_{i}^{y}>1 will be pushed away from the other modality (their repulsive force are stronger than the attractive force) while those for which S i < 1 S_{i}<1 will be pushed towards the other modality. Interestingly, points for which S i y > 1 S^{y}_{i}>1 are those that are close to the other modality ( Fig. ˜ 5 top), so gradient descent will reduce the variance in the direction of the initial gap vector ( Fig. ˜ 5 bottom). The following theorem formalizes this intuition.

Figure ˜ 5 illustrates Theorem ˜ 3.1 . The embeddings begin with equal variance in all directions, but training first shrink the variance in each modality along the initial gap direction g → \vec{g} , resulting in no variance in the gap.

We now prove that once there exists a direction along which both modalities are constant, gradient descent will converge to a solution where both the local gap vectors g i g_{i} and the global gap vector g → \vec{g} are orthogonal to both modalities. Our result similar to that of [ 43 ] but it highlights the crucial role of double-stochasticity: convergence to a nonzero, orthogonal gap vectors requires that the two matrices Q x , Q y Q^{x},Q^{y} are doubly-stochastic , i.e. S j x = S j y = 1 S_{j}^{x}=S_{j}^{y}=1 . While both matrices Q x , Q y Q^{x},Q^{y} are singly stochastic by definition, only in certain point configurations will they also be doubly stochastic. One such case is when the nearest neighbor matching between the two sets is a perfect match so each x i x_{i} is assigned to exactly one y j y_{j} and vice versa.
Empirically, we have found that in the later stages of gradient descent, double stochasticity does indeed hold approximately but this is not true in the initial iterations ( Fig. ˜ 6 left).

Together, Theorem ˜ 3.1 and Theorem ˜ 3.2 explain the dynamics seen in Fig. ˜ 4 . At first, according to Theorem ˜ 3.1 , learning begins by shrinking the variance in the direction of the gap. Once this happens and there is no variance in the direction of the gap, then according to Theorem ˜ 3.2 the gradient in the vertical dimension of the top figure remains zero and the loss continues to decrease by moving the points only in the horizontal direction until the loss reaches a value close to zero with a constant, orthogonal gap vector. In the bottom figure, the initial iterations reduces the variance in the gap direction and subsequent iterations decrease while moving in the other two directions.

This phenomenon of an orthogonal gap has previously been noticed to hold empirically for multi-modal models trained on various datatypes [ 42 ] . We term this the global orthogonality assumption and formally define it as:

Our theorems provide an explanation for the origin of assumption 3.3 provided that the initialization of the two modalities takes the form of two, separated and concentrated clusters. Figure ˜ 6 (right) shows that this is indeed the case - the distance between the two modalities is much larger than the inner modality distance for random initializations of CLIP on real data. This has already been shown to be the case for multi-modal models, a phenomenon termed the "cone effect" [ 11 ] .

To summarize, multi-modal models are commonly initialized in non-intersecting tight clusters with an existing gap. According to our analysis, under these conditions initial training dynamics decrease the variance of these clusters in the direction of the initial gap, while subsequent iterations maintain the gap while aligning the two modalities only in directions orthogonal to the gap, thus creating global orthogonality at convergence. For completeness, we provide more empirical support for our assumptions in Appendix ˜ D and further discussion of the dimensionality collapse assumption in Appendix ˜ C .

sections.heading: 3.3The Modality Gap Decreases Robustness
sections.text:
We define robustness ("Rob") as the probability that when adding noise sampled from some distribution 𝒫 \mathcal{P} to the embeddings of modality 𝒳 \mathcal{X} , the nearest neighbor of some y ∈ 𝒴 y\in\mathcal{Y} will not change: Rob ​ ( 𝒳 , 𝒴 , 𝒫 ) = 𝔼 y ∼ 𝒴 , ϵ ∼ 𝒫 ​ [ 𝟙 NN y , 𝒳 ) = NN ( y , 𝒳 + ϵ ) ] \text{Rob}(\mathcal{X},\mathcal{Y},\mathcal{P})=\mathbb{E}_{y\sim\mathcal{Y},\epsilon\sim\mathcal{P}}\left[\mathds{1}_{\text{NN}y,\mathcal{X})=\text{NN}(y,\mathcal{X}+\epsilon)}\right] (10) Notice that in the zero shot classification setting, this definition of robustness captures semantically meaningful changes - any change of nearest neighbor from image embedding to text, necessarily changes the classification of that image.

We now ask how does the existence of the gap relate to the robustness of the models to noise in the embedding space. Figure ˜ 7 provides intuition. In this figure, there are two captions and the space of images that are close to one caption or another is defined by a linear decision boundary. Any noise added to the text embeddings changes the decision boundary between the two classes. In the case of a gap between the cluster of image embeddings and text embeddings, as images are further away from the texts, their sensitivity to change in the classification decision boundary grows. We prove this formally in the following theorem.

For full proof and generalization for the cases of | 𝒳 | > 2 |\mathcal{X}|>2 and general noise we refer the reader to Appendix ˜ A . Theorem ˜ 3.4 proves that under the orthogonality assumption, when moving one modality towards the other by translating it with the global gap vector then the embedding spaces become more robust and less sensitive to noise. This provides us with clear motivation to close the gap.

We note that while the theorem is phrased in terms of the global gap vector, any vector that is orthogonal to modality 𝒳 \mathcal{X} will suffice, as long as translating y → \vec{y} with it will decrease the distance between y → \vec{y} and the mean of modality 𝒳 \mathcal{X} .

In real applications, robustness is not the only desirable property of a system and a method that increases robustness while significantly decreasing the clean accuracy, will be of limited interest.
We therefore strive to understand when does closing the gap have no effect on downstream performance, therefore solely improving robustness. Since most downstream tasks such as zero shot classification or text to image retrieval are based on cross modal nearest neighbors, we ask when can we guarantee that the nearest neighbors will not change when varying the gap.

Under the orthogonality assumption 3.3 such a nearest neighbor preserving direction is simply the gap vector g → \vec{g} . Therefore Theorem ˜ 3.5 ensures us that narrowing the gap along this direction would not change performance of the model on downstream tasks such as zero shot classification.

sections.heading: 4Algorithm
sections.text:
From Sec. ˜ 3 we conclude that although the contrastive loss maintains the gap created at initialization throughout the training, it is desirable to close the gap in order to improve robustness. Theorems 3.4 and 3.5 provide us with a tool to do so - move the modalities towards each other along the global gap vector, as under assumption 3.3 this provably does not affect performance on downstream tasks when there is no noise at all.

Since in practice the gap vector is not completely orthogonal to the modality subspaces (see Appendix ˜ D ), we suggest projecting the gap vector to be exactly orthogonal to the affine subspace of the modality being retrieved before closing the gap. This is done by computing the principal components V V of the modality being retrieved (texts in case of classification) and projecting the gap g → \vec{g} to the orthogonal complement of the components: g → ′ ← g → − V ​ V T ​ g → \vec{g}^{\prime}\leftarrow\vec{g}-VV^{T}\vec{g} (11) Afterwards, the modality being retrieved is moved towards the mean over the other modality by translating 𝒴 ← 𝒴 − g → ′ \mathcal{Y}\leftarrow\mathcal{Y}-\vec{g}^{\prime} , therefore decreasing the distance of any query point to the retrieved modality, as per Theorem ˜ 3.4 . Theorem ˜ 3.5 assures us that closing the gap in the direction of g → ′ \vec{g}^{\prime} would not affect performance on any downstream tasks that relies on computing nearest neighbors. In the appendix, we also provide an extension to this algorithm that allows moving in directions that are not perfectly orthogonal to each modality (see Appendix ˜ G ).

Contrary to previous attempts at closing the gap, our method requires no retraining or finetuning of the models [ 17 , 16 ] , or the training of new ones (such as the prior network in [ 22 , 18 ] ). Our method is also general and does not adhere to a specific setting [ 12 ] - any task using cross modal nearest neighbors could benefit from it.

sections.heading: 5Experiments
sections.text:
We turn our attention to understanding how well our theory and algorithm work in practice for various real world models and datasets. We follow [ 11 ] and experiment on standard zero shot classification and image-text retrieval tasks. We create embeddings of commonly used, pretrained, multi-modal models using using the openclip library [ 4 ] .

To measure robustness, we empirically estimate:

R ​ ( α ) = 1 K ​ ∑ i = 1 K 1 | 𝒴 | ​ ∑ y ∈ 𝒴 𝟙 NN ( y , 𝒳 + α ⋅ g → ) = NN ( y , ( 𝒳 + ϵ i ) + α ⋅ g → \displaystyle R(\alpha)=\frac{1}{K}\sum_{i=1}^{K}\frac{1}{|\mathcal{Y}|}\sum_{y\in\mathcal{Y}}\mathds{1}_{\text{NN}(y,\mathcal{X}+\alpha\cdot\vec{g})=\text{NN}(y,(\mathcal{X}+\epsilon_{i})+\alpha\cdot\vec{g}} (12)

Where K K is the number of samples of noise, ϵ i ∼ 𝒫 \epsilon_{i}\sim\mathcal{P} is a sample from the noise model 𝒫 \mathcal{P} (such as quantization or gaussian noise) and α ∈ ℝ \alpha\in\mathbb{R} is a scalar controlling the amount of the gap that we close. We set K = 1 K=1 (e.g. when there is a single deterministic quantization) unless stated otherwise.

sections.heading: 5.1Robustness Under Controlled Noise
sections.text:
Our first set of experiments attempts to verify our theory and algorithm in the presence of controlled noise added to the embedding space. We add varying degrees of independent Gaussian noise with zero mean and variance σ 2 \sigma^{2} to each text embedding x → ∈ 𝒳 \vec{x}\in\mathcal{X} and translate the text embeddings by α ⋅ g → ′ \alpha\cdot\vec{g}^{\prime} according to our algorithm ( Sec. ˜ 4 ). We do this for different values of α \alpha therefore expanding or closing the gap. For each change in the gap, we measure both accuracy and robustness ( Eq. ˜ 12 ) averaging over K = 100 K=100 samples of noise.

We test our algorithm on various models, in both zero shot classification and visual question answering (VQA) on the A-OKVQA dataset [ 26 ] , following the evaluation protocol of Ghosal et al. [ 3 ] for CLIP-like models. Results are displayed in Fig. ˜ 8 - when applying our algorithm under Gaussian noise robustness greatly increases when closing the gap across all models and tasks, and greatly decreases when expanding the gap. As noise increases, robustness is lower but still improves when the gap is closed. See Appendix ˜ E for similar results on other noise distributions.

As predicted by Theorem ˜ 3.5 , the zero shot and VQA accuracy do not change when closing the gap in the direction orthogonal to the affine subspace of texts.

sections.heading: 5.2Robustness to Quantization
sections.text:
We continue and test our theory and algorithm on noise distributions that apply to real world applications. A setting of uncorrelated noise is that of quantization [ 14 ] . While many forms of model quantizations exist, we limit ourselves to the simple case of post-training quantizing of the embedding vectors alone, a setting relevant to tasks in which the embeddings were calculated in advance e.g. retrieval augmented generation (RAG) [ 38 ] . We close the gap to varying degrees and then quantize the text and image embedding vectors to different numbers of intervals between a constant range of [ − 3 , 3 ] \left[-3,3\right] (as all embeddings are on the unit hypersphere), and measure robustness.

Empirically, we find that since quantization noise does not always have zero mean, it is better to close the gap until the gap is minimized in the quantized embedding space rather than the original one. Figure ˜ 9 displays results for these experiments. As can be seen, robustness is greatly improved when closing the gap before quantizing the embedding vectors.

sections.heading: 5.3Robustness to Rephrasing
sections.text:
We now turn our attention to text rephrasings - noise that is added in the input space rather than embedding space - which we’ve investigated thus far. We find that in this setting our theoretical assumptions do not hold - specifically, the noise in the embedding space that results from the rephrasing has non-zero mean and tends to lie entirely within the text subspace, with little to no variance in the direction of the gap (and see Appendix ˜ F ). This is not surprising as models that are trained on large datasets are expected to be invariant to various input noises, such as text rephrasing.

We find that our algorithm can indeed be used for such cases. For each question in the A-OKVQA dataset, we simulate a sample of random noise by sampling 3 random wrong answers and a random correct answer, exact details are provided in Appendix ˜ F . Since in this setting the answers are randomly selected, we focus on measuring accuracy under noise instead of robustness, while closing the gap using our algorithm. Results are presented in Fig. ˜ 10 - accuracy under rephrasings greatly increases when the gap is closed using our algorithm, while clean accuracy is unaffected.

sections.heading: 6Discussion
sections.text:
In this paper, we have attempted to provide a comprehensive theory for the formation of an orthogonal gap in multi-modal models. Specifically we showed that when initializing with tight clusters, minimizing the contrastive loss will lead to solutions where a global gap vector separates the two modalities and that this vector will be orthogonal to both modalities. We continued to examine the effect of such a gap in the context of robustness. We prove that in this context, the gap is indeed a bug - as the modality gap increases, the consistency of the retrieval when applying subtle variations to the embedding space is decreased.

Following this theoretical insight, we presented a simple and computationally inexpensive algorithm that improves robustness with hardly any reduction in clean accuracy. Our algorithm could easily be implemented as a post processing step when using multi-modal model embeddings.

Finally, we began examining the effect of our theory and algorithm in the context of real noise, such as quantization and text rephrasing. We view our experiments as a hint to the potential of our algorithm and encourage further investigation as to settings of noise in which our simple algorithm could improve downstream performance.

sections.heading: Acknowledgments
sections.text: This research was supported by a joint Hebrew University IBM Research grant. YW thanks the ISF and the Gatsby Foundation for their financial support.

sections.heading: A.3Proof ofTheorem˜3.4
sections.text:
Now to prove the theorem:

Another way to see this is to note that we can write w ~ = w + η \tilde{w}=w+\eta with η \eta a zero mean r.v. with covariance 2 ​ σ 2 ​ I 2\sigma^{2}I . The retrieval is robust if w ~ T ​ y > 0 \tilde{w}^{T}y>0 . Substituting: w ~ T ​ y = ( w + η ) T ​ y = w T ​ y + η T ​ y \tilde{w}^{T}y=\left(w+\eta\right)^{T}y=w^{T}y+\eta^{T}y (33) From our assumption, w T ​ y > 0 w^{T}y>0 , so robustness will be maintained if w T ​ y > − η T ​ y w^{T}y>-\eta^{T}y . From our assumptions: Var ​ ( η T ​ y ) = 2 ​ σ 2 ​ ‖ y ‖ 2 \text{Var}(\eta^{T}y)=2\sigma^{2}\left\lVert y\right\rVert^{2} (34) Again, according to our assumptions, replacing y → y + g y\rightarrow y+g maintains: w T ​ y = w T ​ ( y + g ) w^{T}y=w^{T}(y+g) (35) since g g is orthogonal to w w , and from Lemma ˜ A.1 : ‖ y ‖ > ‖ y + g ‖ \left\lVert y\right\rVert>\left\lVert y+g\right\rVert (36) Therefore: Var ​ ( η T ​ ( y + g ) ) = 2 ​ σ 2 ​ ‖ y + g ‖ 2 < 2 ​ σ 2 ​ ‖ y ‖ 2 \displaystyle\text{Var}(\eta^{T}(y+g))=2\sigma^{2}\left\lVert y+g\right\rVert^{2}<2\sigma^{2}\left\lVert y\right\rVert^{2} = Var ​ ( η T ​ y ) \displaystyle=\text{Var}(\eta^{T}y) (37) meaning that: P ​ ( w T ​ ( y + g ) < − η T ​ ( y + g ) ) < P ​ ( w T ​ y < − η T ​ y ) P\left(w^{T}(y+g)<-\eta^{T}(y+g)\right)<P\left(w^{T}y<-\eta^{T}y\right) (38) Since we decreased the variance of a zero mean r.v..

sections.heading: A.4Extensions toTheorem˜3.4
sections.text: For completeness, we provide two extensions for the proof, one in the case of perturbation with non-zero mean, and another in the case of a general covariance structure.

sections.heading: D.1Empirical Evidence for Apprximate Double Stochasticity
sections.text: We repeat the experiment measuring S j x , S j y S_{j}^{x},S_{j}^{y} as in Fig. ˜ 6 only for embeddings trained on the unit hypersphere in ℝ 64 \mathbb{R}^{64} . Results are shown in Fig. ˜ 12 - as training progresses, S j x , S j y → 1 S_{j}^{x},S_{j}^{y}\rightarrow 1 meaning that the matrices Q x , Q y Q^{x},Q^{y} become more doubly-stochastic.

sections.heading: D.2Empirical Evidence for Orthogonality
sections.text: For completeness we recompute results from [ 42 ] showcasing the orthogonality of the global gap vector for different models and datasets in Fig. ˜ 13 .

sections.heading: F.1Rephrasing Noise is Correlated
sections.text:
Caption rephrasing, such as replacing the caption "a photo of a X" to "an image of X", tends to result in correlated noise in the embedding space. Intuitively, this is because in the input space, rephrasing can be seen as subtraction of a constant input - the string "a photo of a" followed by addition of the constant string "an image of". When these changes are applied to all inputs, it can be expected that the change in the embedding space would either not exist (if the model is completely robust to such changes) or be consistent.

To show this, we conduct the above experiment on Imagenet. Assume all N N class names (texts) are embedded with the prefix "a photo of a", resulting in the text embedding matrix X X . We create a noise text embedding matrix, X ~ \tilde{X} by embedding all class names with a different prefix (e.g. "an image of"). We repeat for 400 different caption templates, and calculate the empirical noise covariance. Define the noise to be M = X ~ − X M=\tilde{X}-X . The covariance C C is then: C = ( M − 1 N ​ 1 → ​ 1 → T ​ M ) T ​ ( M − 1 N ​ 1 → ​ 1 → T ​ M ) C=(M-\frac{1}{N}\vec{1}\vec{1}^{T}M)^{T}(M-\frac{1}{N}\vec{1}\vec{1}^{T}M) (49) To measure how much the noise is correlated, we simply measure the norm of all off-diagonal elements relative to the forbenius norm of the covariance matrix: d ​ ( C ) = ‖ C − diag ​ ( C ) ‖ F ‖ C ‖ F d(C)=\frac{\left\lVert C-\text{diag}(C)\right\rVert_{F}}{\left\lVert C\right\rVert_{F}} (50) This is of course equal to zero in the case that the noise is uncorrelated and d ​ ( C ) = 1 d(C)=1 when completely correlated. Fig. ˜ 15 measures d ​ ( C ) d(C) for different caption rephrasings for different models on Imagenet. As can be seen, in all cases d ​ ( C ) ≈ 1 d(C)\approx 1 meaning the noise is indeed extremely correlated.

sections.heading: F.2Rephrasing A-OKVQA
sections.text:
In the multiple choice VQA setting, we follow the protocol of Ghosal et al. [ 3 ] : given a question Q Q , for each possible answer A i A_{i} for i ∈ [ N A ] i\in\left[N_{A}\right] we embed the text that is the concatenation of Q + A i Q+A_{i} resulting in N A N_{A} text embeddings. We chose the estimated answer via cosine similarity with the image embedding. We evaluate on the entire A-OKVQA validation set.

In order to rephrase a correct answer, we simply swap the correct answer A j A_{j} with each of the "direct answers" options for that particular question in the dataset. Each question has up to 10 different possible correct answers, each one constitutes as a rephrasing. To replace the wrong answers we sample from the entire answer bank (not including the correct answers).

Notice that in this case our measure of robustness isn’t meaningful as the "noisy" wrong answers are completely different texts, therefore we shouldn’t expect the model to consistently predict the same wrong answer when adding this type of noise. Therefore, under this setting we focus on consistency w.r.t. right answers, which is similar to measuring accuracy when adding noise. If accuracy increases when closing the gap, the model is more consistent on predicting the right answer, or in other words - robust w.r.t. that answer.

sections.heading: H.1Details
sections.text:
All simulations are done by optimizing randomly initialized embedding vectors using the Eq. ˜ 6 . We use full batch gradient descent with a learning rate of 0.01.

In Fig. ˜ 4 (top) we train unnormalized embedding vectors, directly optimizing Eq. ˜ 6 without a normalization step. We initialize the embeddings sampled from two normal distributions 𝒩 ​ ( μ 1 , 2 , 0.01 2 ⋅ I ) \mathcal{N}(\mu_{1,2},0.01^{2}\cdot I) with μ 1 , 2 = ( 0 , ± 0.5 ) \mu_{1,2}=(0,\pm 0.5) . We use a constant temperature of τ = 0.1 \tau=0.1 and train for 10 7 10^{7} iterations.

In Fig. ˜ 4 (bottom) and Fig. ˜ 16 we initialize 1000 embeddings per modality drawn from a normal distribution 𝒩 ​ ( ± e → 1 , 0.01 2 ⋅ I ) \mathcal{N}(\pm\vec{e}_{1},0.01^{2}\cdot I) with e → 1 \vec{e}_{1} being the first elementary basis vector. All embeddings are normalized as is done in training multi-modal contrastive models [ 20 ] .

sections.heading: H.2Further Experiments
sections.text: We ablate both the dimension, initial distance between modality means and temperature. As stated in the main paper (and shown in [ 27 ] and [ 25 ] ), there exist cases in which training converges to completely aligned modalities. This can happen when double stochasticity does not hold throughout the iterations, and double stochasticity may depend subtly on the temperature τ \tau and the number of iterations. We demonstrate this in Fig. ˜ 17 .

figures.caption: (a) CLIP’s embedding of MS-COCO validation set
(a) CLIP’s embedding of MS-COCO validation set
(b) "a photo of a dog / frog" ∨ \vee "photograph of the dog / frog " × \times

Figure 3 : Three points in each modality in ℝ 2 \mathbb{R}^{2} and the corresponding multi-modal contrastive loss along with the magnitude of the gradient of the loss. Lines connect true pairs. As long as the points satisfy relative alignment - the true pair of any point is also its nearest neighbor - the loss and the gradient magnitude are close to zero, even when there exists a gap.

Figure 4 : The evolution of embeddings using gradient descent on the contrastive loss ( Eq. ˜ 6 ) starting with two tight clusters. Both when using an unnormalized embedding space (top) and when constricting the embeddings to the sphere (bottom) they converge to a solution that has almost zero loss and for which a global gap vector exists and the gap vector is orthogonal to both modalities. We prove that minimizing the contrastive loss will lead to such a solution under certain assumptions. For full training details see Appendix ˜ H .

Figure 5 : (Top:) Two initial embeddings where the bottom embedding is color-coded based on S i y S^{y}_{i} . S i y S^{y}_{i} decreases with distance to the other modality. ( Bottom: ) The training dynamics of a toy model initialized with isotropic Gaussians. Training starts by shrinking variance in the direction of the gap according to Theorem ˜ 3.1 .

Figure 6 : Empirical evidence for the assumptions of our thoeorems on training dynamics Left : The variance of S i x , S i y S_{i}^{x},S_{i}^{y} in log scale throughout training of Fig. ˜ 5 . The values of S i x S_{i}^{x} and S i y S_{i}^{y} approach their means as training progresses, which is 1 1 by definition, meaning that the matrices Q x , Q y Q^{x},Q^{y} are approximately doubly-stochastic. Right : KDE plot of the gap’s norm and the distances between the embeddings and their means calculated over 50 random initializations of CLIP (ViT-B-32) on 5000 randomly sampled images and texts from the Flickr30k dataset [ 10 ] . The within modality distance is much smaller than the distance between the modalities.

Figure 7 : Illustration of the relationship between robustness and the modality gap. Two image embeddings (blue squares) are classified identically when using the black texts. However, once slight noise is added to the texts (red), only the image with a smaller "gap" will maintain its classification.

Figure 11 : (Top: Two set of points that perfectly satisfy dimensionality collapse. The bottom points are color-coded by S i y S_{i}^{y} . Note that S i > 1 S_{i}>1 for points near the center. (bottom: The training dynamics. Despite no variance in the direction of the gap for both modalities, the solution converged to has no gap and is perfectly aligned. This is because in the initial iterations, points near the center are pushed away from the other modality hence destroying the original low dimensionality of each modality.

Figure 12 : We calculate S i x , S i y S_{i}^{x},S_{i}^{y} throughout the training of N = 500 N=500 embedding pairs initialized from a Gaussian distribution with variance σ 2 = 0.01 \sigma^{2}=0.01 and gap of ‖ g → ‖ = 1.8 \left\lVert\vec{g}\right\rVert=1.8 . As training progresses, the values of S i x , S i y S_{i}^{x},S_{i}^{y} are concentrated around their means, which by definition equal 1 1 , making the matrices Q x , Q y Q^{x},Q^{y} more doubly-stochastic.
Figure 14 : Results for different noise distributions and models on CIFAR10. All are normalized to have variance σ 2 = 0.025 2 \sigma^{2}=0.025^{2} . Robustness increases when the gap is closed.

Figure 15 : We compute d ​ ( C ) d(C) according to Eq. ˜ 50 . For all models we tested, with over 400 different captions, d ​ ( C ) ≈ 1 d(C)\approx 1 suggesting the noise is extremely correlated in the embedding space.

Figure 16 : We follow Shi et al. [ 27 ] and learn embeddings 𝒳 , 𝒴 ∈ ℝ 1000 × 512 \mathcal{X},\mathcal{Y}\in\mathbb{R}^{1000\times 512} on the hypersphere using the contrastive loss ( Eq. ˜ 6 ) and gradient descent. While the loss decreases and training converges (left), a major gap remains between the embeddings and orthogonality holds (right, measured using ˜ 3.3 ).

Figure 17 : Top: When training with τ = 1 \tau=1 training converges to a solution without a gap, despite existence of an initial gap and orthogonality. Middle: This is consistent for training in higher dimensions as well - different temperatures have different effects on how much of the gap is closed. When temperatures are ≥ 1 \geq 1 , the gap closes throughout training. In higher temperatures training hardly differs from initialization. When the gap is initialized at zero it remains so for all temperatures.
Bottom: Example of dynamics in ℝ 256 \mathbb{R}^{256} with τ = 10 \tau=10 . The gap closes throughout training as it converges to perfect alignment.

Figure 18 : The zero shot classification accuracy and robustness under noise η ∼ 𝒩 ​ ( 0 , σ 2 ​ I ) \eta\sim\mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^{2}I) , on ImageNet and MS-COCO. Top row: When using Algorithm ˜ 1 with ϵ = 5 % \epsilon=5\% of the variance, decrease in accuracy is negligible ( < 1 % <1\% ) relative to the robustness gained, which can be ∼ 10 % \sim 10\% . Bottom row: As the threshold ϵ \epsilon grows larger, more of the gap is closed, greatly increasing robustness at a negligible cost of accuracy.

Figure 19 : Even when using Algorithm ˜ 1 the drop in R@1 for SigLIP [ 40 ] on image to text retrieval on MS-COCO dataset [ 13 ] is negligible relative to the improvement in robustness for different Gaussian noises (left). Fig. ˜ 19 shows the ranges of the singular value threshold ϵ \epsilon for which the increment in robustness (for Gaussian noise with σ 2 = 0.01 \sigma^{2}=0.01 ) is larger than the decrease in R@1.
